Title/Coaching Level: University of Delaware Collegiate Team Head Coach
question: A skater feeling nervous before warm-up
answer: Nerves are normal since they mean that you care and want to do well. Use them to fuel a good performance.

Title/Coaching Level: National and International coach
question: A skater feeling nervous before warm-up
answer: Feeling nervous is completely normal and understandable. Use the warm-up to settle in and feel comfortable. Always remember you are prepared and to trust your training.

Title/Coaching Level: Figure skating coach; National Senior Solo Dance Medalist; Solo International Pattern Dance National Champion
question: A skater feeling nervous before warm-up
answer: Trust your training, believe in yourself, and just try to be positive.

Title/Coaching Level: US Junior National and National Coach in Ice Dancing
question: A skater feeling nervous before warm-up
answer: Rely on your day-to-day training. It will be challenging to make changes and corrections in that moment. Before warmup, just clear space in your head and click into muscle memory. It will be calming and reassuring if you think about it like that.

Title/Coaching Level: National and International Singles and Solo Dance Coach
question: A skater feeling nervous before warm-up
answer: Have a game plan going into this and stick with that. Trust your training and remember you're prepared and ready for this competition.

Title/Coaching Level: Figure skating coach- skating skills, freestyle, and synchronized skating
question: A skater feeling nervous before warm-up
answer: During the warm up process, it is important to focus on your breathing to help you stay centered. Listening to music will also help you stay in the zone and block out distractions.

Title/Coaching Level: Olympic and World Coach
question: A skater feeling nervous before warm-up
answer: Visualize yourself at your home rink, so you can feel as comfortable as you do at home. Take a deep breath or two to reduce your stress.

Title/Coaching Level: Figure Skating Coach and Choreographer
question: A skater feeling nervous before warm-up
answer: Take some deep breaths and be okay with being nervous. Being nervous makes you human.

Title/Coaching Level: National Junior Solo Dance & International Pattern Dance Medalists; Senior Solo Dance Competitor
question: A skater feeling nervous before warm-up
answer: Trust your training. You have trained everything so hard and are ready for the competition. You know what to do. You just need to let go and do it.

Title/Coaching Level: National and international coach
question: A skater feeling nervous before warm-up
answer: Trust your training. All the work you put in before the competition is good enough. You should be competing what you practiced and not trying to do more.

Title/Coaching Level: University of Delaware Collegiate Team Head Coach
question: A skater overwhelmed right before stepping on the ice.
answer: Break down your program, talk abt each element. Focus on taking it one at time, instead of focusing on full program. Use a mental checklist to check things off

Title/Coaching Level: National and International coach
question: A skater overwhelmed right before stepping on the ice.
answer: Close your eyes and take slow, deep breaths. Take one element at a time and focus on what you are doing in the moment.

Title/Coaching Level: Figure skating coach; National Senior Solo Dance Medalist; Solo International Pattern Dance National Champion
question: A skater overwhelmed right before stepping on the ice.
answer: Take a deep breath, take everything one thing at a time, and just trust your practice.

Title/Coaching Level: US Junior National and National Coach in Ice Dancing
question: A skater overwhelmed right before stepping on the ice.
answer: We all feel like that. Rely on your everyday performance. Focus on expressiveness and going through the movements you do everyday.

Title/Coaching Level: National and International Singles and Solo Dance Coach
question: A skater overwhelmed right before stepping on the ice.
answer: Trust your training and trust your preparation. Take it one thing at a time and remember to breathe in the program.

Title/Coaching Level: Figure skating coach- skating skills, freestyle, and synchronized skating
question: A skater overwhelmed right before stepping on the ice.
answer: Let’s visualize the program then walk it through on the ground. Listening to the program while walking it through will also help keep a skater focused.

Title/Coaching Level: Olympic and World Coach
question: A skater overwhelmed right before stepping on the ice.
answer: All your training has you ready, that's why we do it. Rely on muscle memory, but you still have to make it happen at that moment. So try to be mentally present.

Title/Coaching Level: Figure Skating Coach and Choreographer
question: A skater overwhelmed right before stepping on the ice.
answer: Close your eyes and imagine what you would do after everything is set and done. Breathe and find something that keeps you calm.

Title/Coaching Level: National Junior Solo Dance & International Pattern Dance Medalists; Senior Solo Dance Competitor
question: A skater overwhelmed right before stepping on the ice.
answer: Trust your training, and take one thing at a time. It's really easy to get overwhelmed with the picture of the whole program. Focus on one thing at a time and stay focused on what you are doing.

Title/Coaching Level: National and international coach
question: A skater overwhelmed right before stepping on the ice.
answer: Focus on actionable items such as little reminders for each element. Focus on what you can do to make elements work rather than worrying about all the what ifs. Focus on what's in your control to make a better outcome.

Title/Coaching Level: University of Delaware Collegiate Team Head Coach
question: A first-time competitor experiencing anxiety
answer: Talk them through what to expect. Remind them that this is for fun and think about why you do this. It was your choice to compete, so enjoy it and have fun

Title/Coaching Level: National and International coach
question: A first-time competitor experiencing anxiety
answer: Feeling anxious is expected, but I am so confident in your ability to go out there and do your best. I am so proud of the effort you have put in up until this point and I know when you finish your program you will feel so proud that you did it.

Title/Coaching Level: Figure skating coach; National Senior Solo Dance Medalist; Solo International Pattern Dance National Champion
question: A first-time competitor experiencing anxiety
answer: The stakes are low, so go out there and have fun. This is a great opportunity to show everything you've been practicing.

Title/Coaching Level: US Junior National and National Coach in Ice Dancing
question: A first-time competitor experiencing anxiety
answer: It's a completely normal feeling. It gets easier for advanced competitors, but it never goes away completely. It's definitely okay to feel nervous as long as you don’t let it be the primary focus of what you are doing.

Title/Coaching Level: National and International Singles and Solo Dance Coach
question: A first-time competitor experiencing anxiety
answer: It's exciting for you to be competing today. Feeling nervous is completely normal and is something that everyone experiences from first-time competitors up to Olympic-level athletes. You are ready and prepared, and just enjoy the opportunity to go out and skate with no one else on the ice and to show everyone what you've been working on.

Title/Coaching Level: Figure skating coach- skating skills, freestyle, and synchronized skating
question: A first-time competitor experiencing anxiety
answer: Take each element one at time. If you make a mistake, let it go and just move on to the next element.

Title/Coaching Level: Olympic and World Coach
question: A first-time competitor experiencing anxiety
answer: Have fun and enjoy! Think about performance, instead of worrying about elements.

Title/Coaching Level: Figure Skating Coach and Choreographer
question: A first-time competitor experiencing anxiety
answer: It's okay to make mistakes. This is to try and see if you enjoy doing this. You don't have to be perfect and just give it your best.

Title/Coaching Level: National Junior Solo Dance & International Pattern Dance Medalists; Senior Solo Dance Competitor
question: A first-time competitor experiencing anxiety
answer: It's just your first time, and it can only go up from here. So go out there, have fun, and enjoy it!

Title/Coaching Level: National and international coach
question: A first-time competitor experiencing anxiety
answer: Soak in the experience, be aware yourself in the moment and try your best to enjoy the process.

Title/Coaching Level: University of Delaware Collegiate Team Head Coach
question: A skater worrying about disappointing their coach or parents
answer: This is your journey and we are here every step. No matter how you skate, we'll be proud of you for going out there and trying your best. Remember why you skate, it's for yourself.

Title/Coaching Level: National and International coach
question: A skater worrying about disappointing their coach or parents
answer: Your coach and parents only want you to do your best, we are your biggest cheer leaders. The effort you put into practice everyday is what makes us proud of you, your 4 minutes out on the ice at competition does not define you. If you make a mistake in competition, we would be heart broken for you, but never disappointed.

Title/Coaching Level: Figure skating coach; National Senior Solo Dance Medalist; Solo International Pattern Dance National Champion
question: A skater worrying about disappointing their coach or parents
answer: Don't worry, focus on how you're skating, and be present in the moment. Don't let the end result overwhelm you.

Title/Coaching Level: US Junior National and National Coach in Ice Dancing
question: A skater worrying about disappointing their coach or parents
answer: I understand it's a fear, but I can't imagine a coach being disappointed in the skater. Coaches always want you to do your best whether it's successful or not. As long as you try your best and do what you’ve trained for, everyone will be happy.

Title/Coaching Level: National and International Singles and Solo Dance Coach
question: A skater worrying about disappointing their coach or parents
answer: Remind yourself why you started skating. This journey is about yourself and what your goals are. As a coach, I may be disappointed for you but never disappointed in you because everyone goes out to try their best. Sometimes it's your day but sometimes it isn't and that is ok. It isn't going to be perfect every single time. Just go out and try and focus on why you want to skate and your own motivations, not worrying about how anyone else feels about your performance. At the end of the day, it's up to you and how you feel you performed.

Title/Coaching Level: Figure skating coach- skating skills, freestyle, and synchronized skating
question: A skater worrying about disappointing their coach or parents
answer: Remember why you skate. Do not worry about what anyone else thinks because at the end of the day, you skate because you love it and it’s how you identify yourself.

Title/Coaching Level: Olympic and World Coach
question: A skater worrying about disappointing their coach or parents
answer: Skate for yourself. Find the inner reasons why you love to skate. Don't do it for anyone else.

Title/Coaching Level: Figure Skating Coach and Choreographer
question: A skater worrying about disappointing their coach or parents
answer: Nothing could disappoint me. You could make mistakes, but as long as you get back up and keep going, that's all that matters.

Title/Coaching Level: National Junior Solo Dance & International Pattern Dance Medalists; Senior Solo Dance Competitor
question: A skater worrying about disappointing their coach or parents
answer: There's no reason to think about that. The only person who should feel anything about their skating is yourself. Focus on yourself and what you need to do at the moment. You can't really disappoint your coach and parents because they come to support you. Be happy about what you are going to do out there.

Title/Coaching Level: National and international coach
question: A skater worrying about disappointing their coach or parents
answer: You are taking ownership of your own skating. It has nothing to do with anyone else other than yourself. When you compete or skate, you are the one who is in control. All those outside factors have nothing to do with you in that moment.

Title/Coaching Level: University of Delaware Collegiate Team Head Coach
question: A skater competing at a new level for the first time
answer: Remind them that it's your first year in level. With so many new things like levels and elements, whatever is, it's just a starting point. You will grow into this level, so don't expect highest score or highest placement.

Title/Coaching Level: National and International coach
question: A skater competing at a new level for the first time
answer: All of the work has already been done behind the scenes. You belong here and you are ready to take on this new challenge.

Title/Coaching Level: Figure skating coach; National Senior Solo Dance Medalist; Solo International Pattern Dance National Champion
question: A skater competing at a new level for the first time
answer: Have an open mind, think positive thoughts, and just trust your practice and training.

Title/Coaching Level: US Junior National and National Coach in Ice Dancing
question: A skater competing at a new level for the first time
answer: It's easier said than done. I wouldn't have much expectations especially for the initial few competitions. At a new level, you may have new element, longer programs, or new artistic direction. Just go for it and see how it goes.

Title/Coaching Level: National and International Singles and Solo Dance Coach
question: A skater competing at a new level for the first time
answer: It's exciting to get to compete at a new level. It's challenging to move up, but you are ready. You get the opportunity to go out and show some new skills and show all the hard work you put in at practice. You get to try new things out on competitive ice. It might not go perfectly but that's ok since you're still learning and growing.

Title/Coaching Level: Figure skating coach- skating skills, freestyle, and synchronized skating
question: A skater competing at a new level for the first time
answer: Let’s treat this as a new experience. Skate your heart out and try your best. this will allow us to set new goals for the next competition.

Title/Coaching Level: Olympic and World Coach
question: A skater competing at a new level for the first time
answer: A new level is a challenge, and there's no expectations on the athlete because no one knows where you will fit in at the new level.

Title/Coaching Level: Figure Skating Coach and Choreographer
question: A skater competing at a new level for the first time
answer: You are competing at this level because you are ready. Have some confidence that you have the capability of being here and you belong to this level.

Title/Coaching Level: National Junior Solo Dance & International Pattern Dance Medalists; Senior Solo Dance Competitor
question: A skater competing at a new level for the first time
answer: You worked hard for the new level. Go out there with no expectations. Put everything you have on the ice and leave nothing.

Title/Coaching Level: National and international coach
question: A skater competing at a new level for the first time
answer: There are years intentionally for growing, learning and pushing yourself. A new level is like an uncharted territory for a skater. It’s okay to not know where things gonna end up. Focus on your growth throughout the season, and not so much on what your idea of success.

Title/Coaching Level: University of Delaware Collegiate Team Head Coach
question: A skater worried about scores or judging
answer: That is out of ur control, so focus on what u can control and try to put out ur best skate and something you're proud of. Let everything else fall into place bc it's out of ur hands.

Title/Coaching Level: National and International coach
question: A skater worried about scores or judging
answer: Try to focus on what you can control. Judging is a big part of our sport, but it is completely out of our control. As long as you focus on putting your best program out there the scores should reflect that.

Title/Coaching Level: Figure skating coach; National Senior Solo Dance Medalist; Solo International Pattern Dance National Champion
question: A skater worried about scores or judging
answer: Do the best you can do since the scoring is not in your control. Just focus on what you can control and take everything one element at a time.

Title/Coaching Level: US Junior National and National Coach in Ice Dancing
question: A skater worried about scores or judging
answer: Don’t get caught up in individual scores too much. Scores can be subjective to some degrees. Panels will be different at each competition. You should be aware of the trends of your scores over the season, but don't focus on one specific score.

Title/Coaching Level: National and International Singles and Solo Dance Coach
question: A skater worried about scores or judging
answer: The judges and their scores are not in your control. You don't have control over how the judges feel about your performance. At the end of the day, what matters is how you feel about your performance and how you feel about your progress towards your goals. As long as you feel like you got what you wanted out of that performance then it's a successful day.

Title/Coaching Level: Figure skating coach- skating skills, freestyle, and synchronized skating
question: A skater worried about scores or judging
answer: Focus on one element at a time. We cannot control the outcome score wise, but we can control how we feel when we skate and we can control how good we feel once we finish.

Title/Coaching Level: Olympic and World Coach
question: A skater worried about scores or judging
answer: You can't control that. You can control what you do, but you can never control the score.

Title/Coaching Level: Figure Skating Coach and Choreographer
question: A skater worried about scores or judging
answer: There is only so much you can control. Just put yourself out there and put your best foot forward. We can worry about the scores with a grain of salt.

Title/Coaching Level: National Junior Solo Dance & International Pattern Dance Medalists; Senior Solo Dance Competitor
question: A skater worried about scores or judging
answer: You can only control how you skate. Worrying about scores or judging is out of our hands. Go out there and do your best.

Title/Coaching Level: National and international coach
question: A skater worried about scores or judging
answer: Focus on the items that you are in control of. For example: what do you need to do to make your elements successful? What do you need to do to make your features incredibly clear? Everything else is out of your control. Once you do your job, you can get off the ice saying you did the most you could to have the best possible outcome.

Title/Coaching Level: University of Delaware Collegiate Team Head Coach
question: A skater comparing themselves to other competitors
answer: Everyone's skating journey is different, and everyone is on a different timeline. Don't focus on others, focus on your own journey. Think about why u do this instead of comparing urself to someone else.

Title/Coaching Level: National and International coach
question: A skater comparing themselves to other competitors
answer: All athletes are not meant to look and skate the same. Your skating style is what makes you special. Try to focus on you and put blinders on to the other competitors.

Title/Coaching Level: Figure skating coach; National Senior Solo Dance Medalist; Solo International Pattern Dance National Champion
question: A skater comparing themselves to other competitors
answer: It's only a competition with yourself, you're only trying to beat your last best score. It's important just to stay positive and present and take everything one thing at a time.

Title/Coaching Level: US Junior National and National Coach in Ice Dancing
question: A skater comparing themselves to other competitors
answer: That's the nature of competitions and human nature to a degree. You can't control what your competitors do at the event. It's important to keep your eyes on what you are doing and try to make your individual program the best you can. Look for personal improvement rather than comparing to others.

Title/Coaching Level: National and International Singles and Solo Dance Coach
question: A skater comparing themselves to other competitors
answer: The other skaters and competitors aren't something you can control and it's not up to you how the other skaters perform. Just focus on yourself, your plan, and what you can control in the moment, which is how you approach your competition both mentally and physically.

Title/Coaching Level: Figure skating coach- skating skills, freestyle, and synchronized skating
question: A skater comparing themselves to other competitors
answer: Focus on your own progress and your own journey. You are very prepared for the competition, just go out and have fun.

Title/Coaching Level: Olympic and World Coach
question: A skater comparing themselves to other competitors
answer: You are doing this for yourself, not for anyone else. You have no control over other skaters, but you have complete control over what you are about to do. Stay focused on yourself and your own performance. We want every competitor to do a good job, but we still want to come out on top.

Title/Coaching Level: Figure Skating Coach and Choreographer
question: A skater comparing themselves to other competitors
answer: Do you compare top skaters? If you don't do it to them, why do you do that to yourself?

Title/Coaching Level: National Junior Solo Dance & International Pattern Dance Medalists; Senior Solo Dance Competitor
question: A skater comparing themselves to other competitors
answer: All you can control is yourself. You are only capable of doing your best, so you should focus on yourself, because at end of day what you put out there is what matters, not what other people do.

Title/Coaching Level: National and international coach
question: A skater comparing themselves to other competitors
answer: Even though competition does feel like you are headed against other skaters, remember that the person you are truly competing against is yourself. Try to improve yourself and look for a better protocol from competition to competition, as opposed to chasing other skater's scores.

Title/Coaching Level: University of Delaware Collegiate Team Head Coach
question: A skater who made a mistake mid-program
answer: You did great job recovering because it is a very tough thing to recover from a mistake, and you had the mental toughness to keep going. Don't focus on one mistake because there were lots of other good things from performance.

Title/Coaching Level: National and International coach
question: A skater who made a mistake mid-program
answer: Forget about it. Losing focus can make one mistake turn into multiple mistakes. Focus on what is left in the program and do them to the best of your ability. You are human, mistakes happen, learning how to move past them is what matters.

Title/Coaching Level: Figure skating coach; National Senior Solo Dance Medalist; Solo International Pattern Dance National Champion
question: A skater who made a mistake mid-program
answer: Congratulations! Good job! If you're upset about it, it's ok, it happens, we can get it next time.

Title/Coaching Level: US Junior National and National Coach in Ice Dancing
question: A skater who made a mistake mid-program
answer: Everybody is a person, and no matter how much you prepare, little things can happen and people can make mistakes. That's just part of competition. I understand the disappointment, but you will grow from how to navigate something doesn't feel perfect.

Title/Coaching Level: National and International Singles and Solo Dance Coach
question: A skater who made a mistake mid-program
answer: Think about the positive things that you did out there. Sure this is what may have gone wrong in the moment, so you can approach and fix it next time. You can work on it at home, but even though there was a mistake in the program, there were still all of these other things you performed really well.

Title/Coaching Level: Figure skating coach- skating skills, freestyle, and synchronized skating
question: A skater who made a mistake mid-program
answer: Take a deep breath and let it go. It’s okay to make a mistake. Refocus your mind on the next element and just have fun.

Title/Coaching Level: Olympic and World Coach
question: A skater who made a mistake mid-program
answer: You never know what's gonna happen during the program. I would be very proud of you if you could recover after the mistake, because it's much harder to bring a program back that has a problem than to keep a good program going.

Title/Coaching Level: Figure Skating Coach and Choreographer
question: A skater who made a mistake mid-program
answer: Mistakes happen. You can't always be perfect. We wanna aim for that, but you are not ever gonna always be perfect. Whatever mistake was made, you should work toward trying to lessen the chances of happening again.

Title/Coaching Level: National Junior Solo Dance & International Pattern Dance Medalists; Senior Solo Dance Competitor
question: A skater who made a mistake mid-program
answer: If it's early on in the season, it's okay, probably due to nerves. It's normal to make mistakes. When you go back, rework on the section. Make sure that any possible error in feeling is fixed, so you will be ready for the next event.

Title/Coaching Level: National and international coach
question: A skater who made a mistake mid-program
answer: It's human to make mistakes, and it's more about how you recover from the mistake and how you let it go. Use it as an opportunity to grow and learn. You will learn more from recovering from a mistake than skating perfectly every time.

Title/Coaching Level: University of Delaware Collegiate Team Head Coach
question: A skater who worries about stamina
answer: Let ur training speak for itself. If you have put in the training and listened to coach's guidance about run throughs and cardio training, it will help you get through program. Nothing changes from training to competition. Focus on other factors of the program like performance instead of just will I make it.

Title/Coaching Level: National and International coach
question: A skater who worries about stamina
answer: You have done so many run-throughs leading up to this moment. I have no concerns about you being able to get through this. Your mind will give up way before your body does, you are strong and you can do this.

Title/Coaching Level: Figure skating coach; National Senior Solo Dance Medalist; Solo International Pattern Dance National Champion
question: A skater who worries about stamina
answer: Take a deep breath, you just have to do what you have to do. There is nothing you can do right before you compete for stamina, so just believe in yourself and tell yourself you got this.

Title/Coaching Level: US Junior National and National Coach in Ice Dancing
question: A skater who worries about stamina
answer: You can’t really make adjustments at that point. Just do your best to get through the programs. It's normal for everyone to not feel physically 100% at competitions, that's why you repeat and train your program all the time.

Title/Coaching Level: National and International Singles and Solo Dance Coach
question: A skater who worries about stamina
answer: Your program is only so long, so just take it one thing at a time and keep pushing through. Focus on the element you're doing and not on how you're feeling in the moment.

Title/Coaching Level: Figure skating coach- skating skills, freestyle, and synchronized skating
question: A skater who worries about stamina
answer: You are well trained for this program. Focus on one element at a time and keep breathing throughout  the program.

Title/Coaching Level: Olympic and World Coach
question: A skater who worries about stamina
answer: Remember to breathe through the program and you will be fine, because your body is trained and ready for the event.

Title/Coaching Level: Figure Skating Coach and Choreographer
question: A skater who worries about stamina
answer: Push through as much as you can. Breathe and could work on increasing the stamina through future lessons and practices, and work on increasing it to be more ready for the next competition.

Title/Coaching Level: National Junior Solo Dance & International Pattern Dance Medalists; Senior Solo Dance Competitor
question: A skater who worries about stamina
answer: Adrenaline will help in general. Try to perform it, because when you perform your program, you tend to get a little more lost in the feeling of it and you won't be too worried about getting through it.

Title/Coaching Level: National and international coach
question: A skater who worries about stamina
answer: You've had all the training you put in, and you don’t need to do more than you've practiced. You just need to put out what you've been training.

Title/Coaching Level: University of Delaware Collegiate Team Head Coach
question: A skater disappointed after finishing their skate
answer: Ask them how it felt and why are you disappointed. Many other good things I saw in your performance. It's ok to have these feelings (validate them), since you cant always be perfectly happy about a skate, but in the end, you did the best as you could and it can only go up from here.

Title/Coaching Level: National and International coach
question: A skater disappointed after finishing their skate
answer: Your feelings of being upset are completely valid. It's okay to be disappointed, but do not let this skate define you. You are human, we are in a high pressure environment, and mistakes happen. It is a learning experience and will only make you stronger.

Title/Coaching Level: Figure skating coach; National Senior Solo Dance Medalist; Solo International Pattern Dance National Champion
question: A skater disappointed after finishing their skate
answer: Congratulations! It's okay to feel disappointed. We can learn from this and do better next time since this is not the end be all.

Title/Coaching Level: US Junior National and National Coach in Ice Dancing
question: A skater disappointed after finishing their skate
answer: Even the best skaters in the world can be disappointed with their performance from time to time. You put so much effort into it, so it’s understandable to not feel your performance is the best. It's important to take it for what it is. If you made a mistake during the program, it's to a degree not in your control and you have to be ok with it as a possibility of competing.

Title/Coaching Level: National and International Singles and Solo Dance Coach
question: A skater disappointed after finishing their skate
answer: Reflect on why you're disappointed and what about your skate you're disappointed about. Remember what parts and elements you've improved from last time and think about what you might do differently next time, whether it's preparing differently, warming up differently, or having a different mental approach in the future. Next time you can try something different and have a different result.

Title/Coaching Level: Figure skating coach- skating skills, freestyle, and synchronized skating
question: A skater disappointed after finishing their skate
answer: It is okay to have a bad day on the ice. Let’s set new goals and focus on the road ahead.

Title/Coaching Level: Olympic and World Coach
question: A skater disappointed after finishing their skate
answer: You could use this a learning tool. This event didn't go the way you wanted, but you could roll up your sleeves and figure out what went wrong and what to work on to make sure you get stronger and better next time. No one gets through this sport without having a bad competition.

Title/Coaching Level: Figure Skating Coach and Choreographer
question: A skater disappointed after finishing their skate
answer: Why are you disappointed? Find 3 positives things you did in your program.

Title/Coaching Level: National Junior Solo Dance & International Pattern Dance Medalists; Senior Solo Dance Competitor
question: A skater disappointed after finishing their skate
answer: Regardless of how you skated, you did your best. You can't always be perfect, especially in the early season. There are bound to be skates you are unhappy with. Go back, work hard on the mistakes, and fix it for next time.

Title/Coaching Level: National and international coach
question: A skater disappointed after finishing their skate
answer: It’s okay to be upset. How to move forward from it is more important. How are you going to change your training or your mindset so this outcome doesn't happen again?

Title/Coaching Level: University of Delaware Collegiate Team Head Coach
question: A skater who fell or messed up during warm-up
answer: This is a good thing since you can get it out way right now. When you do in program, you'll be more aware at that area and it's a good reminder.

Title/Coaching Level: National and International coach
question: A skater who fell or messed up during warm-up
answer: It's very normal to feel rattled after a mistake or fall in the warm-up. Try to shake it off and remember it was just a fluke, you have done it so many times successfully. Visualize what you need to do to make it successful.

Title/Coaching Level: Figure skating coach; National Senior Solo Dance Medalist; Solo International Pattern Dance National Champion
question: A skater who fell or messed up during warm-up
answer: Take a deep breath, it's in the past. It doesn't matter for anything, so just refocus and get ready to put your best out. Take everything one thing at a time.

Title/Coaching Level: US Junior National and National Coach in Ice Dancing
question: A skater who fell or messed up during warm-up
answer: You really have to compartmentalize that and not think about it. It can be stressful and can throw you off, but move on from it as quickly as possible. You are not going through your program in a perfect space during warmup. There's no sense in getting caught up in it.

Title/Coaching Level: National and International Singles and Solo Dance Coach
question: A skater who fell or messed up during warm-up
answer: That was the warm up and not the competition. You know how to do these elements that you've trained, prepared, and put in the work. Just go out and trust your training and take things one at a time because your body knows what to do.

Title/Coaching Level: Figure skating coach- skating skills, freestyle, and synchronized skating
question: A skater who fell or messed up during warm-up
answer: The warm up is the perfect time to work through the mistakes. You got out the nervous jitters and now it’s time to perform.

Title/Coaching Level: Olympic and World Coach
question: A skater who fell or messed up during warm-up
answer: That's why you have the warm-up, to get the nerves out. When music starts, rely on your training and focus on performance. Elements are trained and prepared, so focus on the performance and let the elements happen. It will be a more successful skate and everyone will enjoy watching it.

Title/Coaching Level: Figure Skating Coach and Choreographer
question: A skater who fell or messed up during warm-up
answer: Get it out of your system, and work out why the mistake happened. Try to fix it quickly then and there. It's ok if you make the same mistake at competition, just address it again at future practices and work on more consistency down the road.

Title/Coaching Level: National Junior Solo Dance & International Pattern Dance Medalists; Senior Solo Dance Competitor
question: A skater who fell or messed up during warm-up
answer: Shake it off. The warmup is the warmup. No need to be worried in that moment. Focus on getting back up and getting through the warmup. Figure it out after that.

Title/Coaching Level: National and international coach
question: A skater who fell or messed up during warm-up
answer: Warm up is chaotic. A slip or stumble there just means you got it out of the way. It helps you refocus on yourself and what you are trying to accomplish in those few mins.

Title/Coaching Level: University of Delaware Collegiate Team Head Coach
question: A skater who skates last in the event
answer: Don't worry about your order since order won't impact what you do. No matter when you skate, you put out what you trained to perform. Don't let it overwhelm what you have to do out on ice.

Title/Coaching Level: National and International coach
question: A skater who skates last in the event
answer: Don't watch your other competitors, go into a warm area where you can sit and focus. Sometimes the build up is the hardest part. Try to quiet your mind, listen to music if that helps, take deep breaths and visualize. Two skaters before you, start to get up and walk around.

Title/Coaching Level: Figure skating coach; National Senior Solo Dance Medalist; Solo International Pattern Dance National Champion
question: A skater who skates last in the event
answer:
Skating last because of placement: It's a privilege to skate last, so take it in and embrace it
Take a deep breath since it isn't something that can be controlled. Focus on what you can control, which is skating a good program.

Title/Coaching Level: US Junior National and National Coach in Ice Dancing
question: A skater who skates last in the event
answer: I suggest, after the warmup, not to watch too much of what's going on prior to your skate. Maybe go to the hallway or step outside for a minute. Just focus on your program coming up, not on who's competing on the ice ahead of you.

Title/Coaching Level: National and International Singles and Solo Dance Coach
question: A skater who skates last in the event
answer:
Skating last is a privilege especially if it's reverse order for placement, so you should be proud of your efforts for the previous program.
Skating order doesn't impact you because you've trained and prepared and it isn't something you can control. Know how to handle the time between the warmup and your competition and find what works best for you. Keep your mind active and don't focus on competing at this time, but also know when to lock in and get ready to get back on the ice. Also have a plan of what to do during the IJS judging period for the previous skater to get your feet back under you.

Title/Coaching Level: Figure skating coach- skating skills, freestyle, and synchronized skating
question: A skater who skates last in the event
answer: Let’s walk through the program and listen to good music to keep our muscles warm and our mind focused.

Title/Coaching Level: Olympic and World Coach
question: A skater who skates last in the event
answer:
You can't really pick your skating order. It normally means you did well prior to that.
If it is just a random draw, you have no control over that. Stay warmed up and don't make a big deal out of it. Keep your mind off of it for as much as possible.

Title/Coaching Level: Figure Skating Coach and Choreographer
question: A skater who skates last in the event
answer: Skating order doesn't necessarily matter in long run. Just breathe, put on headphones and listen to some music. Stay warm and get through it. You can do it!

Title/Coaching Level: National Junior Solo Dance & International Pattern Dance Medalists; Senior Solo Dance Competitor
question: A skater who skates last in the event
answer: It is okay because you've trained your program at all hours of the day and under whole different circumstances. You are prepared to go and attack this program.

Title/Coaching Level: National and international coach
question: A skater who skates last in the event
answer: It's a privilege if it's based on ranking: you earned the opportunity to be there and you worked towards this. Make the most of this opportunity. In general, skate order truly doesn’t affect how you are going to skate because you are still in control of your own skating.

Title/Coaching Level: University of Delaware Collegiate Team Head Coach
question: A skater who skates first in the event
answer: This is great because you get to set the tone for whole event. Go out there and show everyone why you're the standard that everyone else has to skate up to.

Title/Coaching Level: National and International coach
question: A skater who skates first in the event
answer: Prioritize the elements you want to do on your warm-up, most likely you will not get to do all of them. Once the one minute warning is called, take a slow lap around the rink to catch your breath and prepare to compete.

Title/Coaching Level: Figure skating coach; National Senior Solo Dance Medalist; Solo International Pattern Dance National Champion
question: A skater who skates first in the event
answer: You can't control it, so just take easier warm up. Try not to let it block you mentally and keep a positive outlook. Skate order doesn't matter that much, so just look past it and don't make a big deal out of it.

Title/Coaching Level: US Junior National and National Coach in Ice Dancing
question: A skater who skates first in the event
answer: Skating first can be stressful, so you need to have a game plan. Have a wish list of the things you would like to warmup. Allot yourself a minimum downtime before your skate. Have some breathing time and settling time. Budget time for water and regroup.

Title/Coaching Level: National and International Singles and Solo Dance Coach
question: A skater who skates first in the event
answer: It is basically what you do everyday at home: skate first after your warmup. Just treat it like what you would normally do with your run through at home. It's great you don't have to sit around and wait for everyone else to finish.

Title/Coaching Level: Figure skating coach- skating skills, freestyle, and synchronized skating
question: A skater who skates first in the event
answer: Let’s treat it like a normal practice. Take your time and focus on your breathing inbetween the elements.

Title/Coaching Level: Olympic and World Coach
question: A skater who skates first in the event
answer: You have no control over your skating order. Make sure your body is really warmed up prior to your warm-up on ice. Pick the most important things that make you feel grounded during on ice warm-up. Use that time to get comfortable and get into the ice.

Title/Coaching Level: Figure Skating Coach and Choreographer
question: A skater who skates first in the event
answer: Skating order doesn't matter. One upside is that you get over it faster, so then you can watch your friends and others if you like.

Title/Coaching Level: National Junior Solo Dance & International Pattern Dance Medalists; Senior Solo Dance Competitor
question: A skater who skates first in the event
answer: If it's in the first warmup group, stay relaxed, open up the event. There's no expectations, just go have fun. If it's in the later warmup groups, stay calm, get your stuffs done in the warmup and take the last min to breathe and relax. You get to get it over with first.

Title/Coaching Level: National and international coach
question: A skater who skates first in the event
answer: You probably never wait anyway at your home rink. Try to find comfort in staying on the ice and going as soon as you are ready and warmed up. In reality, skating first is very similar to how you train at home.

Title/Coaching Level: University of Delaware Collegiate Team Head Coach
question: A skater struggling with confidence
answer: You are trained and ready. Nothing is different from practice, so just trust your trainings and trust yourself. Go out and have fun because true joy for what you do shows confidence on ice.

Title/Coaching Level: National and International coach
question: A skater struggling with confidence
answer: Confidence has to come from you, but when you're not feeling confident just remember that you can do this because you have done this. You've done great programs in practice and countless competitions before this. You are so capable of anything you put your mind to and  I know that because I have seen it.

Title/Coaching Level: Figure skating coach; National Senior Solo Dance Medalist; Solo International Pattern Dance National Champion
question: A skater struggling with confidence
answer: Trust your practice and try your very best. The most important thing is effort, and the end result isn't always the final goal.

Title/Coaching Level: US Junior National and National Coach in Ice Dancing
question: A skater struggling with confidence
answer: To a degree, everyone struggles with confidence at competitions, regardless of how many times you’ve competed, and everyone has insecurities. Experience and repetition definitely builds confidence. Trust your day-to-day training and preparation.

Title/Coaching Level: National and International Singles and Solo Dance Coach
question: A skater struggling with confidence
answer: You have put in all the work and hours. Trust your training and repetitions. You can do these things, and you just need to trust yourself.

Title/Coaching Level: Figure skating coach- skating skills, freestyle, and synchronized skating
question: A skater struggling with confidence
answer: You are very well prepared for the competition. Do not worry about the outcome. Make sure you focus on smiling and having fun.

Title/Coaching Level: Olympic and World Coach
question: A skater struggling with confidence
answer: Focus on yourself and set some goals for yourself that are achievable. Build on it for the next time.

Title/Coaching Level: Figure Skating Coach and Choreographer
question: A skater struggling with confidence
answer: Think about your strengths whether it's jump, spins, skating skills or choreography, etc. Remember that you have strengths not just weaknesses.

Title/Coaching Level: National Junior Solo Dance & International Pattern Dance Medalists; Senior Solo Dance Competitor
question: A skater struggling with confidence
answer: Everybody goes out on the ice to do the same thing: to perform the program, to put out what they worked hard on and to get feedback on it. No one will make fun of you. Everyone is here to support each other. We are doing the same sport at the end of the day, so just go out there and have fun!

Title/Coaching Level: National and international coach
question: A skater struggling with confidence
answer: Stay in the headspace that you are in control of how you skate. The training you have put in is what you need to put out at competition. What you have been doing in practice is enough. You have done a million times at home and you can do it again here - it's just another day and another run through.

Title/Coaching Level: University of Delaware Collegiate Team Head Coach
question: A skater distracted by the crowd
answer: Focus and worry about what you're doing. Don't get distracted by outside factor and focus on what you have to do.

Title/Coaching Level: National and International coach
question: A skater distracted by the crowd
answer: Focus on you and the work you have to do. Pick a judge to perform to and try to tune everything else out.

Title/Coaching Level: Figure skating coach; National Senior Solo Dance Medalist; Solo International Pattern Dance National Champion
question: A skater distracted by the crowd
answer: Try to stay present and focus on your breath and the element you're doing. Focus on what you can control since you can't control the crowd.

Title/Coaching Level: US Junior National and National Coach in Ice Dancing
question: A skater distracted by the crowd
answer: When you go out there, focus on the surface of the ice and focus on what you are doing when the music plays.

Title/Coaching Level: National and International Singles and Solo Dance Coach
question: A skater distracted by the crowd
answer: Try to stay in the present when you compete. Stay focused on what you are doing and the element you are executing. If you have "keywords" for your program, stick with those. It's okay to acknowledge and feel the crowd at times, but use it to push yourself forward in the performance.

Title/Coaching Level: Figure skating coach- skating skills, freestyle, and synchronized skating
question: A skater distracted by the crowd
answer: Take a deep breath before you begin your program and picture yourself in your home rink. Do not look for anything around you- just focus on the next element and performing.

Title/Coaching Level: Olympic and World Coach
question: A skater distracted by the crowd
answer: If you focus on what you do and your performance, you won't hear the crowd or see them. Don't block them out completely because they can get you excited and help you. Enjoy the crowd, but don't be fearful.

Title/Coaching Level: Figure Skating Coach and Choreographer
question: A skater distracted by the crowd
answer: Who you will be looking for in the crowd? You know they're sitting there. You should be doing this mostly for yourself. Even if you want to keep looking towards the crowd, keep in mind that you are showing what you have practiced for and prepared for. Don't try to skate for someone else.

Title/Coaching Level: National Junior Solo Dance & International Pattern Dance Medalists; Senior Solo Dance Competitor
question: A skater distracted by the crowd
answer: Try to drown the noise out. There are tons of noises that can be made during the program. You can't always control what background noises are going on, so just focus on yourself.

Title/Coaching Level: National and international coach
question: A skater distracted by the crowd
answer: Try to stay in the mindset of one thing at a time, of treating your element list like a grocery list, and make sure to check things off. Focus on task at hand and what's next, rather than outside distraction.

Title/Coaching Level: University of Delaware Collegiate Team Head Coach
question: A skater who tends to overthink
answer: Focus on specific things you need to do. Go through elements and give quick little checkpoints and reminders. Remember, don't over-fixate on one element, focus on the total performance.

Title/Coaching Level: National and International coach
question: A skater who tends to overthink
answer: Try to visualize and focus on one element at a time. Don't think about the "what-ifs", instead think about what you are going to do, and what corrections you are going to apply, to make your competition and training session as successful as possible.

Title/Coaching Level: Figure skating coach; National Senior Solo Dance Medalist; Solo International Pattern Dance National Champion
question: A skater who tends to overthink
answer: Fake it till you make it because confidence is important since you can always tell. Faking it can help build confidence, and remember to be present and take a deep breath.

Title/Coaching Level: US Junior National and National Coach in Ice Dancing
question: A skater who tends to overthink
answer: Overthinking is usually a function of wanting to do your best. Focus on what you do in the moment, eliminate extra thoughts and focus on one thing at a time. Mentally divide your programs into segments, have an elements list, and check off those things one by one.

Title/Coaching Level: National and International Singles and Solo Dance Coach
question: A skater who tends to overthink
answer: If you have keywords for your elements, try to focus on that. Find a way to distract your thoughts. You could play games or think about something silly that happened at your rink this week, or do anything to get your mind off of competing.

Title/Coaching Level: Figure skating coach- skating skills, freestyle, and synchronized skating
question: A skater who tends to overthink
answer: Take a deep breath and focus on one element at a time. Use one keyword before each skill to remind you of the task at hand.

Title/Coaching Level: Olympic and World Coach
question: A skater who tends to overthink
answer: Your worst enemy is overthinking. Sports can be challenging if you overthink too much. Rely more on your muscle memory and preparation. Find something simple to think about such as a performance cue.

Title/Coaching Level: Figure Skating Coach and Choreographer
question: A skater who tends to overthink
answer: Grab something you have such as your AirPods. Focus on it and describe it: color? how does it feel? how heavy? Try to narrow down and focus onto one thing, so the mind doesn't go all over the place.

Title/Coaching Level: National Junior Solo Dance & International Pattern Dance Medalists; Senior Solo Dance Competitor
question: A skater who tends to overthink
answer:
Try to distract yourself, find something that allows you to take a moment.
If you need to be in the moment, just stay focused on what you can do, one thing at a time. It's going to be fine, because you have trained this so many times at home.

Title/Coaching Level: National and international coach
question: A skater who tends to overthink
answer: There's no need to do more today than you do at home. Competition doesn’t mean more. It's an opportunity to showcase the work you have been doing. Think about what you do in training and not situation you are in.

Title/Coaching Level: University of Delaware Collegiate Team Head Coach
question: A skater who has been having a bad training week
answer: Training is always gonna have ups and downs, with some good weeks some weeks not so much. It is good to experience both types since it helps you to be more ready at competition. You can only perform better from here (your bad training week).

Title/Coaching Level: National and International coach
question: A skater who has been having a bad training week
answer: One week does not define you, you have had many other weeks that were amazing. We all have bad weeks and days, but you can overcome this and choose to not let it bring you down.

Title/Coaching Level: Figure skating coach; National Senior Solo Dance Medalist; Solo International Pattern Dance National Champion
question: A skater who has been having a bad training week
answer: There's good weeks and bad weeks and good days and bad days. Don't let one week define you, trust your training from before and be confident.

Title/Coaching Level: US Junior National and National Coach in Ice Dancing
question: A skater who has been having a bad training week
answer: You have nerves and anxiety prior to competition. A bad training week happens to everyone. Sometimes it can help get jitters out, and help you know you can do it even you are not at 100%. It can make you a little tense but it is what it is. Calm yourself down and trust you can execute when you want to, and do your best.

Title/Coaching Level: National and International Singles and Solo Dance Coach
question: A skater who has been having a bad training week
answer: We all have tough weeks sometimes, and it's okay. You still have all the previous weeks of training before that to leaning into. Some elements may not have been great this week, but it doesn't mean competition won't go well because you have put in time and hours. Maybe adjust some expectations for the event. It's ok because it's not everyone's time all the time. Your learning and progressing is what matters. Hopefully next time you will be more prepared. Knowing you have gone through this tough period will make easier times feel so much better.

Title/Coaching Level: Figure skating coach- skating skills, freestyle, and synchronized skating
question: A skater who has been having a bad training week
answer: You are mentally and physically prepared for the competition. Let’s get the nerves out at practice so you can put your best foot forward at the competition.

Title/Coaching Level: Olympic and World Coach
question: A skater who has been having a bad training week
answer: As long as your training prior is solid, a few bad days don't matter. It's good to get the nerves out prior. Rely on your training,  and it's ok to make mistakes during training.

Title/Coaching Level: Figure Skating Coach and Choreographer
question: A skater who has been having a bad training week
answer: Use this as a learning opportunity: timing doesn't always happen in our favor. Things happen. Even the best of the best have bad days and weeks. You just need to keep pushing through.

Title/Coaching Level: National Junior Solo Dance & International Pattern Dance Medalists; Senior Solo Dance Competitor
question: A skater who has been having a bad training week
answer: The week leading up to the competition is always the worst week because one is nervous and that's when mistakes tend to happen. It's better to get those out now than at competitions.

Title/Coaching Level: National and international coach
question: A skater who has been having a bad training week
answer: It's typical for this to happen. It's a habit of overthinking. Treat it as an opportunity to refocus on important things. Use it to help you be aware of the hotspots and trouble areas and keep the positive reinforcement on those things.

Title/Coaching Level: University of Delaware Collegiate Team Head Coach
question: What is one piece of advice you consistently give competitive skaters that you believe has the greatest impact?
answer: Trust yourself and just go out and enjoy it. Remind yourself why you do this and just focus on that. If you go out there and focus on enjoying what you do, you'll be able to skate well.

Title/Coaching Level: National and International coach
question: What is one piece of advice you consistently give competitive skaters that you believe has the greatest impact?
answer: Before you even get to the competition the hard work is done. You've put in so many training hours so you can be at your best in this moment. All that is left is to show everyone what you are capable of and enjoy it. Trust your training, I know you can do this.

Title/Coaching Level: Figure skating coach; National Senior Solo Dance Medalist; Solo International Pattern Dance National Champion
question: What is one piece of advice you consistently give competitive skaters that you believe has the greatest impact?
answer: Fake it till you make it. Everyone may seem very confident, even if they're all faking it, so just act confident even if you don't feel that way. Control what you can control, and focus in on things you can control. Don't let any other external factors impact ur experience and performance.

Title/Coaching Level: US Junior National and National Coach in Ice Dancing
question: What is one piece of advice you consistently give competitive skaters that you believe has the greatest impact?
answer: Be in the moment. When your music comes on, connect with what you are doing in that moment. Don’t think ahead about the end of the program, what other competitors did on ice before you, your score, or how others might think about you. The preparation is done and just connect with what you are doing in that moment.

Title/Coaching Level: National and International Singles and Solo Dance Coach
question: What is one piece of advice you consistently give competitive skaters that you believe has the greatest impact?
answer: Trust yourself and your training. Make sure you stay focused on what you can control. Take one element at a time, stay locked into the present and don't let your thoughts run rampage. Make sure you take measured approach and trust yourself.

Title/Coaching Level: Figure skating coach- skating skills, freestyle, and synchronized skating
question: What is one piece of advice you consistently give competitive skaters that you believe has the greatest impact?
answer: Skate for you. Skate because you love it. Don’t worry about the outcome. Just enjoy the process! There will always be more opportunities if this one wasn’t your favorite.

Title/Coaching Level: Olympic and World Coach
question: What is one piece of advice you consistently give competitive skaters that you believe has the greatest impact?
answer: Focus on the performance you are giving because if you try to enjoy your work and the time you put it, then it's your time to shine. That's why you do all the work at home, so you could enjoy your time at the competition.

Title/Coaching Level: Figure Skating Coach and Choreographer
question: What is one piece of advice you consistently give competitive skaters that you believe has the greatest impact?
answer: When you are nervous, just accept it. In general, when people try to fight how nervous they are, the anxiety increases. Accept that it happens instead of pushing it away. Learn to work with it and work through it.

Title/Coaching Level: National Junior Solo Dance & International Pattern Dance Medalists; Senior Solo Dance Competitor
question: What is one piece of advice you consistently give competitive skaters that you believe has the greatest impact?
answer: Perform. Skating is such a beautiful thing and everybody is so different. You just need to go out there, enjoy what you are doing and have fun with it. Don't get too caught up in scores and placements because it tends to make you loose focus on what skating really is, which is an art.

Title/Coaching Level: National and international coach
question: What is one piece of advice you consistently give competitive skaters that you believe has the greatest impact?
answer: There's no reason one can't show up to a competition most prepared. One thing you can bet on is the work you put in beforehand. When you stand on ice and your music is about to start, you can say to yourself that you’ve done everything you can to be prepared. This will carry you further and give you confidence.
